We started a little over a year ago with alternate-education-inspired summer camps, and have since grown into a space that focuses on four key themes - 21st-century skills, civic sense, self-awareness, and global awareness. column48:
One of the most challenging and impressive projects I worked on was building an automated invoicing tool for the Less-Than-Truckload (LTL) business vertical with 3,000 clients. Invoicing was fully manual, dependent on clients sharing order details and rate cards, which often led to mismatches and disputes. This caused delays of up to 45 days in completing invoicing cycles.

The challenge was both technical and operational. On the technical side, we had to automate 50,000–60,000 invoices across multiple billing cycles while keeping computation costs low. Initially, generating reports took up to 30 minutes. On the non-technical side, adoption was difficult, as the finance team was accustomed to manual processes, and accuracy was critical to client trust.

I led the product development and rollout in phases—first standardizing order and rate card data, then automating invoice generation, and finally optimizing the system to reduce report downloads from 30 minutes to under 2 minutes.

The results were significant: invoicing timelines dropped from 45 days to 10 days, disputes were eliminated, and half of the invoicing team was redeployed to collections automation. The project not only improved efficiency and accuracy but also freed up resources for higher-value work.

column46:
1. AI-Powered Predictive Parking Navigator for UK Cities: Urban parking in the UK is a persistent challenge especially in unfamiliar areas where spaces are limited, time-restricted, or paid. We waste time circling streets, contributing to congestion and emissions, while councils struggle to optimise space usage.

As a solution, I want to build an AI-powered predictive parking navigator that goes beyond real-time availability. This system uses a blend of live traffic data, historical parking trends, local event schedules (for context, when marathons or events happen many roads are shut and rerouted), weather conditions, and smart infrastructure (like CCTV and lamppost sensors) to forecast parking availability across current and nearby streets.
It should have: 
Predictive parking suggestions before arrival
Dynamic rerouting based on changing conditions
Integration with local council data for temporary zones (This is where I wonder if we can get access to council data)
Voice-activated navigation for hands-free use
Optional incentives for off-peak parking to reduce congestion

This solution reduces search time, eases traffic flow, and supports sustainability goals by cutting idle emissions. It also empowers councils to manage parking more efficiently and respond to demand in real time.

2. I believe there are existing tools, but another area I’m personally interested in is using AI to streamline stock market research. I find it quite time-consuming to analyse historical data and trends manually, so I’ve been thinking about an app that could do the heavy lifting—reviewing past performance, identifying patterns, and predicting long-term investment potential. It would be a tool to support smarter, data-driven decisions for personal investing.

Looking at where we are today, I genuinely believe AI could take that kind of work much further. With better modelling, real-time data integration, and predictive capabilities, we now have the tools to build far more sophisticated and impactful solutions.

I would also integrate an interactive live chat model using the same avatar, allowing students to ask questions about these new technologies in an engaging way. column48: The most challenging project I have worked on is generating full stack web application from idea within minutes and providing user with deployed application link. The challenging part was maintaining the consistency of the UI elements in every generation, generating backend services on the go based on the screens, and achieving that all within minutes. Initially I used components of our No code platform and defined json format for defining the pages and styles of the application and trained the AI agent on more than 50 samples of json and its repective designs, then the json that agent used to give we fed it to our no code platform component and fetched each components as every component we made were standalone so there were no dependencies. Now to solve the backed issue we used firebase as there we can directly connect to realtime database without using any APIs. And our already existing no code components provided us the consistency in UI. Then to fasten the process we trained the modle on bootstrap components and provided the agent our firebase creds and made it generate html css and js file instead of creating an angular application we generated pure html css js application and that made the whole approach faster.

By the end of this cohort, I want to gain the ability to build complete AI systems — not just call APIs. I want to deeply understand how large language models work, how embeddings are created, and how to fine-tune models effectively using techniques like LoRA, QLoRA, and parameter-efficient training.

I’m also very interested in learning how to build RAG-based and Agent-based AI applications, especially using:
	•	Vector Databases (FAISS / Pinecone / Chroma)
	•	RAG pipelines & retrieval strategies
	•	LangChain / LangGraph style orchestration
	•	Tool calling & reasoning agents

Along with that, I want to explore latest AI trends, such as:
	•	Multimodal models (text + image + speech)
	•	Model quantization to run LLMs efficiently on limited hardware
	•	Synthetic data generation for fine-tuning and evaluation
	•	Guardrails and evaluation frameworks to measure model reliability

By the end of the cohort, my goal is to confidently take an idea from concept → building → deploying a real, production-ready AI application that solves a meaningful problem. Ultimately, I want to grow into a strong AI Engineer, who can design scalable AI solutions responsibly and effectively.

column48:
As our operations grew, goods were constantly moving between different workshops, suppliers, and our main facility. Each batch had to be tracked for quality, payments, and timelines. Coordination was tough: missed shipments, payment delays, and inventory mismatches became a real headache + quality issues

Technical hurdles:
Currently,setting up proper ERP systems to track every item, automate payment reminders, and ensure live inventory monitoring was tough—especially since many vendors used old-school methods. Integrating all the workflows while ensuring zero downtime is taking a lot of trial and error.

Non-technical hurdles:
Convincing my father and team members to adopt new processes, dealing with resistance to change, and building habits around regular updates is a big challenge. + putting up old data on the system is too much work

How I succeeded:
Still working on it - but breaking the problem into small steps—first, digitally tracking goods; then regular check-ins across the team. Results: fewer errors and faster deliveries

I run smart contract security competitions: Blockchain protocols put up their code for a security review, on our platform. Similar to a bug bounty but time boxed. 
Where independent security researchers come and submit vulnerabilities in the code. 
- One of the biggest challenges was that we would get almost 1000 submissions, with more than 90% of them invalid. 
- It takes a lot of resources to judge the findings, cutting thousands of dollars for every security competition we've done. 
- We created an AI agents that is able to review all the submissions within the context of the codebase. Run tests and judge the submission. This did exceptionally well for false positives which is 90% of submissions. 
- This effectively reduced the workload by 80% and expenses by 60%

One of the most challenging projects I worked on was turning around performance for a struggling D2C fashion brand in the UAE that had inconsistent sales, low ad profitability, and creative fatigue. The brand had no clear funnel structure, limited data visibility, and relied heavily on generic creative assets.

Technically, I had to rebuild the entire Meta Ads account structure using a full-funnel OCM (One Campaign Method), segment product categories, set up CAPI with Shopify, and integrate custom events for better attribution. On the creative side, I introduced a lean AI-assisted pipeline using tools like HeyGen, Pletor, and Verv.fm to produce weekly variations of UGC-style video ads, hooks, and statics based on audience feedback loops.

Non-technically, the hurdles were tougher—aligning the founder (who had strong creative opinions) to adopt a test-and-learn mindset, and operating under tight budget constraints with daily fire drills around performance dips.

Over 90 days, we improved ROAS by 3X, reduced CAC by 40%, and built a replicable ad system that didn’t rely on guesswork. The real win was creating a feedback-driven creative ops system that let the brand scale sustainably.

One of the most challenging and impressive projects I worked on was building an AI Real Estate Chatbot and Lead Automation System using n8n and OpenAI APIs.

The goal was to create a chatbot that could talk to potential leads, qualify them based on property needs and budget, and automatically store their data in a CRM all without any manual intervention.

The main challenges were connecting multiple tools (like forms, Gmail, CRMs, and OpenAI) and managing API rate limits, token costs, and prompt design to make the chatbot sound natural while staying efficient. On the non-technical side, I had to understand real estate workflows, how agents handle leads, and what kind of automation would actually save them time instead of adding complexity.

After several iterations, I built a workflow where leads flow from forms → chatbot → CRM → follow-up sequence, all automated within n8n. It taught me a lot about API design, prompt optimization, and system thinking, and gave me confidence to start building AI automations for other industries too.

At Evanostics, a medical device startup in California, I worked alongside PhDs from Stanford and Berkeley — some of the brightest minds I’d ever met. As an engineering student, it was both inspiring and daunting. We were developing cutting-edge diagnostic technology, but despite the brilliance in the room, the company eventually failed.

That experience taught me a lesson I couldn’t have learned in any classroom — that success isn’t just about intelligence or technical depth, it’s about understanding application and timing. You need to know when to zoom in on the details and when to zoom out to see the market, the user, and the bigger picture.

It shaped how I think today: the most valuable people aren’t always the smartest — they’re the ones who can bridge innovation and relevance.

I became an entrepreneur by solving my own problems first, then helping others solve theirs. I started scrappy, tested ideas quickly, and kept going even when plans broke, budgets tightened, or timelines slipped. Along the way I learned that momentum beats perfection, relationships beat cold transactions, and integrity compounds like interest.

KIMP grew from a simple promise to give teams the feel of an in house designer at a fraction of the cost. Systems, not just talent, made it scale. From the first few customers to a global roster, I focused on predictable workflows, fast feedback, and clear ownership of every deliverable.

Real estate pulled me deeper into technology. With Buy.ca, I set out to make home buying more transparent, aligning incentives through programs like Down Payment Boost. Repliers became the data backbone, proving that useful APIs can reshape how agents and investors make decisions. Then came AI products like WOWCall.ai and Agent360, practical tools that save time, capture revenue, and turn missed calls into booked opportunities.

I have bootstrapped, partnered, and reinvested through cycles, always balancing ambition with cash flow reality. I obsess over unit economics, the quality of our people, and the durability of our processes. I have also learned to protect energy, train leaders, and keep teams aligned to simple metrics that actually change outcomes.

Family keeps my why clear. Shalini’s support, our daughters’ resilience, and the responsibilities I carry sharpen my focus. My journey is not a straight line, it is a series of experiments, tough lessons, and quiet wins that stack over time. I still ship, measure, and improve every day, committed to building products that truly help, and companies that last.

The next chapter is about scale, automation, and service. I am building with curiosity, urgency, and care, inviting people to build alongside me.

column48:
Built multiple 8 Figure Subscription businesses.

Problem
Clients needed high quality graphics and videos on a predictable cadence, without hiring in house teams. Freelancers were unreliable. Agencies were expensive. We had to deliver unlimited requests, fast turnarounds, and consistent brand quality for hundreds of clients at once.

Technical hurdles

Workflow engine: Build KIMP360 to move tasks from Requests to In Progress to In Review to Completed, with source files, versions, and brand kits.

Assignment logic: Auto route requests to the right specialist using tags, skills, and current load.

Feedback tools: Point and click annotations, visual diffs, and required comments for low ratings.

AI assist: Use AI to prefill briefs, suggest sizes, and surface templates while keeping designers in control.

Reliability at scale: File handling, version safety, and predictable 24 hour SLAs for simple tasks.

Analytics: Track throughput, queue health, first response time, revision loops, and designer utilization.

Non-technical hurdles

Team design: Create pods with a Project Manager and three designers so clients feel like they have an in house team.

Quality bar: Stand up QA reviewers and clear brand guidelines to prevent rework.

Expectation setting: Educate clients on what “unlimited” means and how to prioritize requests.

Change management: Train hundreds of designers on uniform processes without killing creativity.

What we built

KIMP360 for intake, status, collaboration, and file delivery.

A routing and SLA system that keeps work moving two at a time per subscription.

A QA gate with checklists for brand compliance and print readiness.

A white glove onboarding playbook that captures brand assets once and reuses them everywhere.

Outcome

Turnaround times dropped to a reliable 24 hours for simple graphics, 2 to 4 days for typical videos.

Revisions decreased as briefs improved and QA filtered issues early.

Client retention improved because delivery felt predictable and on brand.

The model became repeatable, with clear unit economics and capacity planning.

Why it is impressive
KIMP blends software, process, and people into a dependable creative system. The win was building an assembly line for creativity that still leaves room for craft.

I started out with a bachelor’s degree in Electrical and Electronics Engineering, which built my foundation in problem-solving and analytical thinking. Right after that, in 2015, I began my professional career with Infosys as a Technology Analyst. That was where I first stepped deeply into the world of Data Engineering and Big Data ecosystems. I worked with technologies like Hadoop, Hive, Spark, PySpark, and gradually expanded into cloud platforms such as AWS, where I built data ingestion pipelines, automated workflows using Airflow, and contributed to data warehouse migrations to Snowflake. Those early years gave me a strong grounding in how large-scale data systems operate and evolve. 
In 2019, I moved to Elsevier into the Data & Analytics team, and this is where my work started having broader business impact. I led the design and delivery of end-to-end data platforms, including real-time pipelines using AWS Glue, DMS, Lambda, and EventBridge. I optimized Snowflake environments for both performance and cost, and improved ETL efficiency by shifting workloads to Polars, which significantly reduced execution time and memory usage. I also built automated monitoring frameworks and contributed to integrating ML-based forecasting models into business workflows. 
Alongside my professional work, I’ve always believed in staying current and advancing my knowledge. I’m a Consultant Metabolic and Bariatric Surgeon working across the NHS and private sector, with a focus on developing personalised, data-driven obesity and metabolic care pathways. My clinical interests span advanced laparoscopic and robotic surgery, metabolic surgery, and digital innovation in patient management.

Over recent years, I’ve been deeply involved in leading AI and digital transformation projects within the NHS — exploring how machine learning can enhance triage, treatment personalisation, and pathway optimisation for obesity and surgical care.

I joined the 100xEngineers Applied Gen AI Master Cohort to bridge the gap between clinical practice and technology learning how to build and apply AI tools that can meaningfully improve patient outcomes, streamline workflows, and future-proof healthcare delivery.
column20:
By the end of the cohort, I aim to develop a Large Language Model (LLM) capable of predicting the effectiveness of specific obesity treatments — from medication and endobariatric therapy to surgery — based on individual patient profiles. The goal is to deliver truly personalised, evidence-based treatment decisions that improve outcomes and optimise resource use.

In the longer term, I hope to evolve this model into a scalable, globally adaptable platform for chronic disease management — enabling AI-driven care and prevention across diverse healthcare systems.

Alongside this, I want to gain the technical and strategic skills to create AI agents and LLM-based tools that will allow me to embark on an entrepreneurial journey at the intersection of medicine, innovation, and technology — ultimately shaping the future of AI in healthcare delivery and prevention.
column32: Entrepreneur Track (I want to build products)
column43:
I want to build and scale AI-powered healthcare products that bridge clinical precision with digital intelligence. Starting with obesity and metabolic disease, my goal is to create LLM-driven decision tools that predict treatment response, personalise care, and optimise outcomes at scale.

Long term, I aim to grow this into a global health-tech venture — one that applies the same predictive and personalised framework to other chronic conditions, driving prevention and early intervention worldwide.

My focus is on translating clinical experience into commercially viable, evidence-based AI solutions, partnering across healthcare, technology, and policy to make intelligent care accessible, affordable, and sustainable.
column45:
Every week, I see patients whose lives could change if we could act earlier, personalise care better, and make complex decisions faster. That’s what drives me.

I’m passionate about using AI to transform how we understand, prevent, and treat obesity and chronic disease — making care more precise, equitable, and effective for every patient.

I believe technology should enhance, not replace, human judgment — giving clinicians the tools to focus on empathy, connection, and outcomes rather than admin and inefficiency.

Ultimately, I care about building a future where AI and healthcare work hand in hand — delivering prevention, prediction, and personalisation at scale, both in the NHS and globally.
column46:
I’m currently exploring how Large Language Models (LLMs) can predict treatment effectiveness and personalise care for obesity and metabolic disease, combining clinical, genetic, and behavioural data to deliver true precision medicine.

A core part of my vision is building AI systems that are inclusive and globally adaptable — designed to account for ethnic, genetic, and environmental diversity, ensuring that predictive models remain accurate and equitable across populations.

I’m also focused on developing interconnected LLMs that learn continuously from one another across medical domains. This would enable AI to uncover cross-disease correlations, forecast future treatment possibilities, and evolve dynamically with real-world data — ultimately creating a self-improving, intelligent healthcare ecosystem.

Beyond clinical use, I want these models to also predict research and treatment efficacy, helping design and adapt clinical trials more safely and efficiently. This could accelerate discovery, reduce risk, and bring effective therapies to patients sooner.

My past work has focused on AI-driven triage, pathway optimisation, and personalisation within NHS bariatric services — practical experience that now shapes my ambition to integrate AI from bedside to bench, and back.
column48:
One of the most challenging and rewarding projects I’ve been working on is the Obesity Pathway Innovation Programme (OPIP) — a national initiative aimed at digitally transforming obesity care pathways across the NHS.

The project involves aligning multiple stakeholders: the Integrated Care Board (ICB), hospital leadership, primary care, and external technology partners — each with different priorities, budgets, and operational constraints. My role has been to bring these perspectives together, communicate a shared vision, and demonstrate how AI-driven digital triage and pathway optimisation can improve outcomes, reduce costs, and expand access.

The non-technical challenges have been significant — from navigating governance structures and funding limitations to addressing data security and change management concerns. On the technical side, the challenge lies in designing a system that integrates clinical, behavioural, and demographic data safely and meaningfully to support both personalisation and scalability.

Although the project is ongoing, progress so far has been encouraging: we’ve secured ICB engagement, mapped digital pathway opportunities, and aligned local hospital teams toward piloting AI-enabled patient stratification tools.

The process has taught me that building AI in healthcare isn’t just about algorithms — it’s about aligning people, systems, and purpose.

I’m a Consultant Metabolic and Bariatric Surgeon working across the NHS and private sector, with a focus on developing personalised, data-driven obesity and metabolic care pathways. My clinical interests span advanced laparoscopic and robotic surgery, metabolic surgery, and digital innovation in patient management.

Over recent years, I’ve been deeply involved in leading AI and digital transformation projects within the NHS — exploring how machine learning can enhance triage, treatment personalisation, and pathway optimisation for obesity and surgical care.

I joined the 100xEngineers Applied Gen AI Master Cohort to bridge the gap between clinical practice and technology — learning how to build and apply AI tools that can meaningfully improve patient outcomes, streamline workflows, and future-proof healthcare delivery.
column20:
By the end of the cohort, I aim to develop a Large Language Model (LLM) capable of predicting the effectiveness of specific obesity treatments — from medication and endobariatric therapy to surgery — based on individual patient profiles. The goal is to deliver truly personalised, evidence-based treatment decisions that improve outcomes and optimise resource use.

In the longer term, I hope to evolve this model into a scalable, globally adaptable platform for chronic disease management — enabling AI-driven care and prevention across diverse healthcare systems.

Alongside this, I want to gain the technical and strategic skills to create AI agents and LLM-based tools that will allow me to embark on an entrepreneurial journey at the intersection of medicine, innovation, and technology — ultimately shaping the future of AI in healthcare delivery and prevention.
column32: Entrepreneur Track (I want to build products)
column43:
I want to lead the integration of artificial intelligence into clinical practice, particularly within obesity and metabolic disease management. My goal is to design and deploy AI-driven clinical decision tools that can personalise treatment, predict outcomes, and improve efficiency across healthcare pathways.

Beyond clinical application, I aim to build and scale AI solutions that can be adapted for global health systems, supporting the prevention and management of multiple chronic diseases.

Ultimately, I want to sit at the intersection of surgery, data science, and entrepreneurship — shaping the next generation of AI-enabled healthcare delivery that is commercially viable, evidence-based AI solutions,predictive, personalised, and preventive.
column45:
Every week, I see patients whose lives could change if we could act earlier, personalise care better, and make complex decisions faster. That’s what drives me.

I’m passionate about using AI to transform how we understand, prevent, and treat obesity and chronic disease — making care more precise, equitable, and effective for every patient.

I believe technology should enhance, not replace, human judgment — giving clinicians the tools to focus on empathy, connection, and outcomes rather than admin and inefficiency.

Ultimately, I care about building a future where AI and healthcare work hand in hand — delivering prevention, prediction, and personalisation at scale, both in the NHS and globally.
column46:
I’m currently exploring how Large Language Models (LLMs) can predict treatment effectiveness and personalise care for obesity and metabolic disease, combining clinical, genetic, and behavioural data to deliver true precision medicine.

A core part of my vision is building AI systems that are inclusive and globally adaptable — designed to account for ethnic, genetic, and environmental diversity, ensuring that predictive models remain accurate and equitable across populations.

I’m also focused on developing interconnected LLMs that learn continuously from one another across medical domains. This would enable AI to uncover cross-disease correlations, forecast future treatment possibilities, and evolve dynamically with real-world data — ultimately creating a self-improving, intelligent healthcare ecosystem.

Beyond clinical use, I want these models to also predict research and treatment efficacy, helping design and adapt clinical trials more safely and efficiently. This could accelerate discovery, reduce risk, and bring effective therapies to patients sooner.

My past work has focused on AI-driven triage, pathway optimisation, and personalisation within NHS bariatric services — practical experience that now shapes my ambition to integrate AI from bedside to bench, and back.
column48:
One of the most challenging and rewarding projects I’ve been working on is the Obesity Pathway Innovation Programme (OPIP) — a national initiative aimed at digitally transforming obesity care pathways across the NHS.

The project involves aligning multiple stakeholders: the Integrated Care Board (ICB), hospital leadership, primary care, and external technology partners — each with different priorities, budgets, and operational constraints. My role has been to bring these perspectives together, communicate a shared vision, and demonstrate how AI-driven digital triage and pathway optimisation can improve outcomes, reduce costs, and expand access.

The non-technical challenges have been significant — from navigating governance structures and funding limitations to addressing data security and change management concerns. On the technical side, the challenge lies in designing a system that integrates clinical, behavioural, and demographic data safely and meaningfully to support both personalisation and scalability.

Although the project is ongoing, progress so far has been encouraging: we’ve secured ICB engagement, mapped digital pathway opportunities, and aligned local hospital teams toward piloting AI-enabled patient stratification tools.

The process has taught me that building AI in healthcare isn’t just about algorithms — it’s about aligning people, systems, and purpose.

To strengthen this practical experience, I completed my Finance degree at Indiana University. 	1.	A GenAI layer on top of Kundan Craft’s manufacturing:
I want to use what I learn to build internal AI tools for our factory and wholesale business – things like an AI assistant that can answer questions on designs, orders and inventory, help with demand forecasting and pricing decisions, and plug into our ERP so we can move from manual follow-ups and Excel to smarter, automated workflows.
	2.	An AI-native experience for Ruvél:
For my brand Ruvél, I want to come out of the cohort with at least one live GenAI product – for example, a custom design copilot where customers can describe what they want and see concepts, or an AI-led styling and recommendation engine that uses our catalog and manufacturing constraints from Kundan Craft to suggest pieces we can actually produce.
	3.	A scalable playbook I can keep using:
I’m also hoping to build a repeatable playbook – from idea → prototype → deployed product – plus a small network of mentors and peers. That way, even after the cohort, I can keep shipping new AI features across both Ruvél and Kundan Craft instead of this being a one-time learning experience.

So overall, I want to leave the cohort with real GenAI products running in my jewelry businesses, not just theory.

column48:
The most challenging and impressive project I’ve worked on so far has been implementing an ERP system at Kundan Craft, our family’s jewelry manufacturing and wholesale business.

For years, everything ran on Excel sheets and WhatsApp – orders, inventory, production status, even gold-rate calculations. Each department had its own file and format, so numbers rarely matched. We were losing time reconciling sheets, over- or under-producing certain designs, and relying heavily on a few people’s “memory” of what was happening on the floor.

The challenge was two-fold:
	1.	Technical:
	•	Choosing an ERP that could handle BOMs for jewelry, WIP, gold weights, and buy-back policies, not just generic SKUs.
	•	Cleaning and migrating messy Excel data (duplicate item codes, inconsistent naming, missing fields).
	•	Designing workflows for orders → production → QC → dispatch → payment, and mapping them correctly in the tool.
	•	Integrating daily gold-rate pricing and margins into the system so we could see profitability per order, not just at month-end.
	2.	Non-technical (and harder):
	•	Most employees had used only Excel or paper their entire lives. Many were genuinely afraid that “the system will catch every mistake” and that they’d be blamed.
	•	Some senior staff felt the old way “worked fine” and saw the ERP as extra work and a threat to their importance.
	•	Different comfort levels with English and software meant training had to be very simple and very patient.

I started by sitting with each team (orders, factory, accounts, dispatch) and mapping their exact current process step by step. Then I designed the ERP flows to match reality as closely as possible instead of forcing a textbook process. I created simple, role-based screens, short Loom-style video demos in Hindi/English, and did multiple small-group trainings on real orders rather than dummy data.

For the first few weeks, I ran Excel and ERP in parallel, so people didn’t feel like they were “jumping off a cliff.” Whenever there was a mismatch, we used it as a learning moment to refine fields, validations, and permissions. I also made sure that early wins were visible – for example, being able to see live WIP and stock for a big client in one click, or quickly checking which orders were profitable at current gold rates.

We’re still improving, but today most core operations run through the ERP. We have cleaner data, fewer surprises, better visibility on orders and cash flow, and employees who were once resistant now ask for new reports and features. For me, the real success wasn’t just the software going live, but getting a traditional, Excel-driven business to trust and use a new system every day.

In 2024, I came across the Poem.Time device, which went viral. It was an eink clock with AI generated haiku for each min of the day. I loved it and I wanted to build something for myself along these lines. 

I was working thru some personal issues at the time, and I wanted to build a minimalist e-ink display that shows me my fav affirmations.  

So, I picked up an ESP32 microcontroller and a 2.13-inch e-ink display. Just purchased it on Amazon after watching a few Youtube videos. On and off for over a year, I played around w the ESP32 micro-controller, learnt how to connect it to a board, and power it. 

I set up the libraries and guides following tutorials form the internet and sometimes very painful searches on forums and help websites on how to get the Arduino code correct. . Each step brought its own challenges—from figuring out pin mappings to wondering why nothing would show up on screen (spoiler: it was the reset pin which was loose).

That first "Hello World" on the e-ink screen felt like magic.

Since then I've worked on cleaning up the loading code, generating e-ink images to go with the text affirmations, and spent some time playing around w 3d prototyping ideas for the device unit. 

My next step is to integrate 


I've had a lot of complex professional achievements, but building this e-ink display from scratch when I had 0 experience with hardware, esp micro controllers made me feel very capable and powerful. I've demo-ed the device to a few builder friends and am working on building a talk-therapy and CBT-focused LLM/model that can support talk-therapy on my device.

column46:
I'm constantly exploring new tools and ideas to test them out and stay ahead. Joining this 100X cohort is itself part of that philosophy—ensuring I can supercharge my agency with AI capabilities.

Here's a recent problem we solved: For performance marketing campaigns, we use Meta's ads library to analyze competitors' video ads—studying their hooks, how they're shot, and their scripts. The typical process involves downloading high-performing ads and mimicking the good parts in our unique way. The problem? LLMs like Claude or GPT can't view videos directly. Having my team download and analyze dozens of videos daily, then explain the crux to GPT to create inspired scripts for our clients, was cumbersome and things got lost in translation.

So we built a simple app using Google AI Studio that takes an MP4 video ad, extracts each frame, and lets you download the sequence as a PDF. This way, when you share the PDF with any LLM, it can see camera angles, scripts (visible through captions), hooks, and overall creative execution. We looked at many online tools but couldn't find anything suitable, so we built our own: https://google-ai-studio-apps-three.vercel.app/

Another idea I explored was running my own e-commerce store selling digital products at d2ctemplates.com. Since we create hundreds of ads every month, I realized that once an ad is made and launched, its life is essentially over for that client. But many other brands could use those templates to test out their communication. For example, "Problem-Solution" is a common format where you show a problem on one side and position your product as the solution on the other. There are many formats like this that pretty much all brands can use.

My idea was to sell open files (PSDs, AI files, After Effects projects, etc.) to brands wanting to quickly get started with their performance marketing journey at a low cost. They could download templates from our website and have someone with basic Adobe knowledge plug and play their text and product images into our ads.

Marketing this proved difficult—I only got about ten orders and ran out of my personal marketing budget, so I had to put this on pause. But it's an example of ideas I've explored and learned from.
column48:
It's difficult to pinpoint just one project because as part of my work, we handle D2C brands across vastly different sectors—skincare, fashion, F&B, pet care, and more. Each brand brings a completely different set of challenges when it comes to performance marketing.

The most challenging and recurring hurdle is becoming as knowledgeable about their product and sector as the client's own team and founders. Only when you have deep domain expertise—say, in pet care—can you find creative and effective ways to market and sell the product. It's not just about running ads; it's about understanding customer pain points, purchase behaviors, product benefits, competitive landscape, and industry nuances at a granular level.

The non-technical challenge is this knowledge acquisition process itself. I need to first become a subject matter expert in each vertical we work with, then translate all that knowledge to my team so they can execute campaigns effectively. This involves studying competitor strategies, understanding customer psychology, and identifying what messaging resonates with each audience.

The technical hurdles involve adapting our marketing frameworks and attribution models to work across these diverse verticals. A person buying a serum for her face doesn't think or behave the same way as someone buying a supplement for her pet. The purchase journey, consideration timeframes, price sensitivities, emotional triggers, and content consumption patterns are completely different. We need to customize our targeting, creative strategies, funnel structures, and ROAS benchmarks for each sector.

How we overcome this: We've built a systematic onboarding process where we deep-dive into client businesses through extensive discovery calls, competitive analysis, and customer research. We create detailed documentation that gets shared with the entire team. We run structured creative tests to learn what works in each vertical, building our own internal playbooks over time. This challenge repeats practically every other week with new clients or campaigns, and successfully navigating it—delivering strong ROI despite the learning curve—is what makes our work both challenging and rewarding

column43:
I want to build a next-generation Workflow Substrate that becomes the underlying operating layer for AI-native IT Service Management. Today’s enterprise workflows—incident triage, change management, ticket routing, approvals, RCA, compliance, audits—are fragmented across email, tickets, spreadsheets, and human judgment. AI agents cannot operate on top of this chaos.

My goal is to create a universal workflow memory and decision substrate that captures every action, signal, context, correction, QC loop, dispute, and outcome in a structured, machine-readable “Episode.” This substrate would power:

Autonomous L1–L3 IT support agents

Automated RCA, change risk scoring, and incident prediction

Gold-standard datasets for RLHF and continuous AI improvement

Human + AI shared workflow execution

Auditable, explainable IT workflows for regulated industries

This becomes the system of record for enterprise actions, enabling enterprises to transition from reactive ITSM to AI-native, proactive, self-optimizing operations.
column45:
I’m passionate about fixing broken enterprise workflows.
Every industry—healthcare, finance, insurance, BPO, government—runs on human workflows that are opaque, slow, error-prone, and impossible to automate or audit. As AI adoption accelerates, these workflows become the biggest blocker.

I care deeply about:

Building transparent, auditable processes

Eliminating operational inefficiencies at scale

Reducing cognitive load and burnout for frontline workers

Creating fair, explainable, AI-driven systems

Turning human expertise into reusable knowledge artifacts

Bringing enterprise AI out of slide decks and into real operations

I want to build infrastructure that upgrades how millions of people work—not with gimmicky automation, but with responsible, high-trust, high-leverage AI systems.
column46:
Current ideas I’m exploring:

Workflow Substrate (core idea): a universal, typed, event-sourced workflow memory layer with Episodes, Decisions, Entity Graphs, QC loops, κ-tracking, agent hooks, and gold-dataset generation.

AI-native BPO automation: using workflow substrate to build autonomous BPO processes in healthcare, insurance, finance, supply chain, and HR.

RLHF 2.0 / Enterprise Gold Datasets: generating structured training data from real business operations.

Multi-agent enterprise systems: ITSM agents, clinical QA agents, compliance agents, credentialing agents.

AI + national-scale credentialing: using AI, blockchain, monitoring, and workflow substrate for continuous credential verification in healthcare.

Past areas I’ve explored:

Building AI assessment engines for healthcare professionals (clinical, behavioral, communication scoring).

Designing multi-agent Copilot architectures for large enterprises.

AI-driven onboarding & compliance flows for regulated industries.

Massive partner integration ecosystems (Vitality, JH, Manulife).

Global credentialing & verification systems for healthcare hiring.

These all compound into the core need for a workflow substrate.
column48:
One of the most challenging projects I led was building an AI-enabled workforce assessment and credentialing system for a national healthcare provider in the UAE. The problem was extremely complex:
10,000+ clinicians from different countries, multiple regulators, inconsistent documentation, manual interviews, no standard QC, and no centralized trust system.

Technical challenges:

Designing multi-agent pipelines (assessment AI, scoring AI, summarization AI).

Building structured evaluation rubrics (clinical, leadership, identity, values).

Handling multilingual, noisy inputs (audio, text, transcripts).

Creating deterministic scoring & QC logic for regulated environments.

Non-technical challenges:

Convincing stakeholders across government, IT, HR, and compliance to change workflows.

Aligning incentives across departments with conflicting priorities.

Managing political sensitivities around automation and fairness.

Creating trust in AI scoring and explainability.

Outcome:

We delivered a system that:

Reduced evaluation time from weeks to hours

Improved QC consistency and transparency

Generated clean, structured datasets for future automation

Became a foundation for a national credentialing modernization roadmap

This experience is what crystallized the Workflow Substrate idea:
enterprises desperately need a structured, auditable, machine-readable layer to capture how humans make decisions—so AI can safely participate in governance-critical workflows.

column48:
One of my most challenging projects was building an ML-based electronic-recognition tool for FabLab Spark. Kids were breaking 6–7 electronic modules per day because they didn’t know how to use them. The goal was to let users upload a picture of any module and get clear instructions, safety notes, and tutorials.

Challenges:

Technical:

Training a model with limited images

Distinguishing between visually similar sensors

Ensuring the UI was simple enough for children

Non-technical:

Understanding why students mishandled electronics

Balancing simplicity with accuracy

Coordinating with educators who had different needs

Success:
I built a functional system that correctly identified common modules like LEDs, motion sensors, gesture units, and more. It reduced daily breakage, saved the instructors time, and made the Spark kit more accessible. It also pushed me to learn ML basics, user-research thinking, and product design under real constraints.

column34:
Content optimization (SEO) for Generative Engines. 

We make it easy for business to optimize their content so as to improve their visibility on generative engines like ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Google GenAI. Similar to SEO, our clients’ brand or product will come up at the top of these generative engine responses.

With the growth of AI powered alternatives to search engines, traditional SEO is failing to capture the users moving to generative engines. Our product solves this problem, thus ensuring a complete reach to the target users.

We are creating a complete platform where the clients could manage their visibility on the generative engines. The MVP includes the following functionalities:
Evaluation: We analyze the client website’s content, check whether they are following the recommended practices and suggest them on how to improve.
Simulation: We simulate how a generative engine will respond after it reads (or is trained on) the content of the client’s website.
Generation: We help the client generate new content that is optimized for best visibility in generative engines.

Overall, we hope to become the one stop solution for managing and improving the generative engine optimization of our clients.

We started working on it, however couldn't proceed to completion. This is on hold.

Category: AI-Powered Marketing Technology (MarTech) for Beauty Industry

The Problem:
Salons struggle with ineffective generic marketing and miss revenue opportunities by not connecting the right services to the right customers at the right time. They lack insights into what's trending in their market and how to capitalize on it.

Our Solution:
An AI-driven marketing platform that analyzes salon customer data against real-time beauty industry trends. We identify which trending services match each customer's demographic profile, automatically generate personalized recommendations with relevant offers, and deliver targeted campaigns that drive bookings.

Key Differentiation:
Unlike existing salon management software that only tracks past behavior, we provide predictive market intelligence—telling salons which services to promote to which customers based on what's trending in their segment. We also recommend when to create strategic offers to capture market opportunities.
Value Proposition:

Transform salon marketing from reactive to proactive, increasing appointment frequency, average ticket size, and customer lifetime value through intelligent, data-driven personalization.

Market Opportunity:
The salon and spa industry generates billions annually, with thousands of independent salons lacking sophisticated marketing capabilities. Our vertical SaaS approach targets this underserved segment with accessible AI technology.
column48:
One of the most challenging and impressive projects I worked on was during my tenure as a Client Delivery Manager, where I was responsible for managing testing milestones and overall delivery for a key client account. The project demanded close coordination across teams, ensuring quality, timely delivery, and alignment with the client’s evolving business goals.

At the outset, our team supported only four applications. Over the span of twelve months, the account scaled rapidly, growing from 35 to 90 team members, and the scope expanded to fifteen applications. This expansion coincided with a period of significant business growth, and we successfully achieved 200 percent revenue growth. The rapid scale-up brought several challenges, both technical and operational.

From a technical standpoint, handling multiple applications introduced complexities in data management and reporting. The client’s expectations for precise and detailed performance reports were high, requiring customized metrics and dashboards. Ensuring consistency and quality across all applications demanded robust coordination between testing teams and development streams.

On the non-technical side, managing communication across a growing team and maintaining alignment with client stakeholders were key challenges. It required setting up effective governance mechanisms, standardizing delivery practices, and ensuring all stakeholders were consistently informed about progress and risks.

From Level 15 to Ground Zero – My Journey Through the Multiverse of Life and Work

My career began right after my MBA in 2007 when I joined TCS as an HR professional. With each step, I learned, grew, and climbed my way up to become a Talent Acquisition Head. Life seemed linear yet fulfilling — full of milestones, awards, and the thrill of leading teams. When I got the opportunity to move onsite to the UK, it felt like reaching Level 15 of the game of life — everything was falling beautifully into place.

Those three years in London were a whirlwind of growth and achievement. I felt invincible, like I had conquered the professional world. But life, as it often does, had a plot twist waiting for me.

A relocation to Germany changed everything. It felt like stepping into an entirely different multiverse — new rules, new language, new challenges. The same me, but in a world where I suddenly didn’t have the tools to play. From Level 15, I crashed down to Level 0 — ground reality.

The rejections started pouring in. My inbox, had more rejection emails than promotion emails. It was humbling — even painful. The confident HR leader was now a learner again, starting from scratch.

I went back to the basics — I began learning German, spending nearly a year just to manage bahsic conversations. Eventually, I found a role that brought back financial stability, but it wasn’t what my heart wanted. It was sales-driven, and each day I found myself wishing for a machine that could live-translate languages — something to bridge the gap that had caused me so much struggle.

Then came another move — this time to Denmark. But unlike before, I was prepared. I carried with me the lessons of two European countries and the resilience I had earned the hard way.

In Denmark, while job hunting, I noticed a pattern — many expat spouses like me were struggling to find meaningful opportunities. That’s when I decided to create a community — a space for expat spouses to network, learn, and support each other’s professional journeys.

Eventually, I found my place again — this time with a UK-based organization, recruiting Tech Talent globally. It felt like I had finally merged all my worlds — HR, technology, global work culture, and my love for learning age new tools.

But my story isn’t just about my career. It’s also about who I am  as person which shapes me a into a better professional, beyond work —
a mother to a teen boy, an unorganized spouse to a highly organized man (a daily comedy show in itself!),
a passionate cook who has deep conversations with fresh fruits and vegetables,
a nature lover who finds peace in long walks,
a daughter and daughter-in-law who believes family is everything,
a sister who’s always just one call away,
and a friend who shows up when it matters most.

At the heart of it all, I’m a learner, connector, and believer — someone who’s fallen, restarted, and kept playing the game of life, one level at a time. I never call it a game finished, because it never truly ends.

When I was employed I was working with a company called eMudhra which was developing a financial tracking tool. This would capture the user's financial transactions automatically through SMS and categorize their spend. 

I was entrusted with figuring out the user's CASH transactions which could not be tracked and it had to be done manually. 

Most of the tools in the market at that time to track cash were riddled with multiple steps of inputs to record one cash transaction. And I was entrusted to build something different that let users input their cash transactions without having to go through many steps.

The technical issue back then was having a single page app solution which was not common. It was more like multi-step, multi-page solutions. So customizing the app from a design and development perspective was hard. 

We built Coin by Emudhra ( This was before Zerodha's coin :) ) . This was a circular UI / UX experience on app. Very low budget and took 18 days to build. But the steps it took for the user input dropped from 15 steps to about 3 steps. No scrolling, in a circular dial there were the major expense categories laid out. And when an expense category was clicked, the center of the dial had sub categories that could be scrolled without leaving the screen ) And then a pop up to enter the amount. And once it was registered, the details of the cash transaction was listed below. 

It was a simple solution to a rather complicated issue and that was an interesting project to work on.

Right after graduation, I faced the international student's struggle to find a role. Despite the challenges, I landed a contract role as a Client Operations Analyst at DHL. As a person of color navigating a new corporate culture, I confronted my fears and learned to thrive, gaining invaluable experience in handling international clients and logistics operations.


My journey quickly accelerated when I proactively reached out to a recruiter and secured a position as a Customer & Operations Analyst at Relay Technologies in London. column48:
One of the most challenging and impactful projects I worked on was during my time at Cargomatic, where I designed and implemented an OCR-driven system to digitize “gate-in” and “gate-out” memos for trucks entering and exiting storage yards.

The problem:
Yard operations relied entirely on handwritten paper memos to record entry and exit timestamps. These memos were critical for:
- tracking container movements
- determining storage time
- calculating accessorial and detention charges
- resolving billing disputes
- reconciling shipment histories

But the process was completely manual, inconsistent, and error-prone. Memos were often:
- lost, smudged, or unreadable
- filled in differently by different drivers
- difficult to match to shipments
- causing major revenue leakage from unbilled storage or incorrect accessorials

The business needed a reliable and scalable way to digitize this entire process.

Technical challenges:
- Developing an OCR pipeline that could read handwritten, low-quality, unevenly scanned memo sheets.
- Handling multiple different memo layouts—no memo looked the same.
- Building preprocessing steps (thresholding, deskewing, noise reduction) to optimize OCR accuracy.
- Creating post-processing logic to validate, map, and correct extracted container numbers, license plates, driver IDs, and timestamps.
- Building an automated data ingestion workflow that fed clean data into analytics and billing systems.
- Designing dashboards to reconcile backdated storage, identify mismatches, uncover revenue leakage, and correct outstanding charges.

Non-technical challenges:
- Yard teams were comfortable with paper and initially resistant to new technology.
- Drivers submitted memos in inconsistent formats, requiring the system to adapt rather than forcing behavioral change.
- Billing, operations, and yard managers had conflicting definitions of “valid” entry/exit timestamps.
- We had to run both paper and digital workflows in parallel during the transition period.

How I succeeded:
- Built a custom OCR solution using Tesseract + image preprocessing that significantly improved handwriting recognition accuracy.
- Added validation logic using regex rules, entity lookups, and fuzzy matching to clean and standardize extracted data.
- Integrated everything into structured databases with automated ETL flows.
- Created Power BI dashboards that allowed operations and billing teams to reconcile charges, track outstanding accessorials, and monitor yard activity in real time.
- Ran training sessions, aligned different teams on data definitions, and incrementally increased adoption until the digital system became the default.

Planned Next Phase (Computer Vision Automation):
As the OCR system matured, the next phase was to install cameras at yard entry and exit points and use computer vision models to:
- automatically detect truck license plates
- read container numbers
- capture precise entry/exit timestamps
- generate real-time yard visibility
- reduce dependency on manual memos entirely

This would have enabled full automation of yard movements using number plate recognition (ANPR) and container ID tracking—pushing the solution toward a smart-yard system with near-zero manual intervention.

Outcome:
- Reconciliation time reduced by 70–80%
- Revenue leakage significantly reduced due to better storage/accessorial tracking
- Billing disputes dropped with clearer digital records
- Yard operations gained real-time visibility

Our execution of a hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) test bed for a run-of-the-river hydropower plant demonstrates advanced integration of simulation and real control hardware systems. By creating a process simulator that models the hydro plant operations combined with actual PLCs, SCADA systems, communication switches, firewalls, and other hardware, you effectively replicated real-world plant behavior, especially in emergency scenarios.

This approach aligns with industry-standard HIL practices used in power plant controller validation, where real controller hardware interfaces with a simulated plant model running in real-time. Your system integration of control, communication, and security devices alongside the simulator provides a comprehensive test environment for functional verification and robustness assessment before field deployment.

Such an HIL test bed enables safe, repeatable testing of control strategies and emergency response without risks to actual physical plants, optimizing performance and reliability. It reflects sophisticated engineering by validating the interaction of software algorithms, hardware signals, network communication, and emergency protocols in a unified setup.

Overall, this is a robust and practical methodology for hydro power plant system validation, widely recognized for reducing costs, enhancing safety margins, and accelerating commissioning schedules in renewable energy and industrial automation projects

Even though I’ve selected B2B SaaS solutions as the category, the core industry I operate in is media and content. I see a huge shift happening as AI becomes a core part of how animation, motion design and video production are done. But right now, the workflows required to create high-quality AI content are extremely fragmented. You have to jump between text-to-image tools, image-to-video models, prompt engineering, custom GPTs, multi-step pipelines and a lot of manual experimentation. Only early adopters like us, who spend hours testing different approaches, can get consistent results.

I want to turn this messy, experimental space into something usable at scale. My goal is to build unified, productized workflows that simplify AI-driven content creation for businesses. This could look like a node-based platform, internal tools, or modular mini-products that package the best AI workflows into a clean, practical system. Since I already run a media and animation studio, I also want to use these tools internally to improve our own speed and efficiency. If these internal workflows prove reliable and repeatable, the plan is to expand them outward into SaaS solutions that other studios, brands and creators can use. In short, I want to bridge the gap between early experimentation and real-world adoption.
column45:
At a fundamental level, I enjoy solving problems and building practical solutions, regardless of the domain. With AI, I feel we’re at an interesting moment where the technology is extremely powerful, but its real-world usability is still limited to early adopters who are willing to experiment heavily. There’s a big gap between what the models can do and what the average person or business can comfortably use.

I’m passionate about bridging that gap. I care about taking the messy, complex parts of new technology and turning them into simple workflows that anyone can use. Whether it’s in content, media, or other fields, I want to build systems that remove friction, unlock more output for people, and help teams do things faster and at higher quality. I like being early on the curve, understanding how things work under the hood, and then figuring out how to translate that into solutions that have real impact at scale.
column46:
Right now, my exploration is centered around building and scaling the new AI vertical inside Lens Media. Since we naturally operate in animation and motion design, the first area I’m experimenting with is AI video generation. I’m trying to figure out how to achieve production-level output using AI, which requires solving problems around character consistency, environment continuity, style control and multi-step pipelines. There’s no single tool that does all of this, so most of my exploration is about stitching together different models, workflows and techniques to reach a reliable final output.

Alongside this, I’m studying how these fragmented workflows can eventually become usable products or internal tools. For example, while learning ComfyUI, I realised that custom LoRAs, internal node-based pipelines and specialised micro-tools could actually become valuable solutions for businesses, not just creative studios. My exploration now is about understanding where AI can genuinely help businesses and how those ideas can translate into scalable offerings in the future.
column48:
One of the most meaningful projects I worked on was a product called Listr. It originally started from a personal problem - I used to bookmark interesting Twitter threads intending to read them later, but the bookmarks would pile up and become overwhelming. While talking to a few users to understand the problem better, I discovered something more interesting: Twitter lists were extremely underused, even though they were a far better way to customise content consumption. The real issue was discovery, because Twitter didn’t provide an easy way for people to find or explore good lists.

This insight shifted the product direction. Instead of solving just bookmarks, I built Listr as a simple no-code platform where users could browse high-quality Twitter lists across domains like crypto, startups, sports and more, curated by me and other credible creators. It allowed people to follow a list and instantly get a personalised feed without having to individually follow hundreds of accounts.

I built the entire product using no-code tools, launched it on Product Hunt, and it took off - it became the #2 Product of the Day, went viral on Twitter, a tech YouTuber made a video on it, and it gained over 8,000 users in the first month. For me, it was validation that even a small solution to a niche but genuine problem can resonate if it’s built with the right insight. A couple of months later, a France-based company working in a similar space acquired Listr. It was a challenging but rewarding experience that reinforced my interest in identifying real user problems and building scrappy but effective solutions.

column48:
The most challenging and impressive project I’ve worked on was building and running large-scale job fairs and internship fairs that connected thousands of candidates with hundreds of startups. The core problem was chaotic hiring. Early-stage companies had high demand but no structured process, and students had zero clarity on what roles existed, how to prepare, or how to stand out. There was no unified system that matched both sides at scale.

The non-technical hurdles were intense. I had to convince companies to participate without an established brand, coordinate with college leaders, manage expectations on both sides, and create a workflow that didn’t collapse under the volume. Every founder wanted different criteria, every campus had different timelines, and no one had patience for long coordination cycles. On top of that, events had to run smoothly even when thousands of students showed up at once.

The technical hurdles came from building and maintaining all the backend workflows required to handle this volume. I had to create registration systems, sorting logic, match-making lists, communication pipelines, and evaluation workflows that could handle thousands of submissions in hours. There was no dedicated platform, so everything had to be stitched together using forms, sheets, automation, and custom scoring frameworks.

I succeeded by designing a scalable, end-to-end system. I created role templates, standardized JD libraries, automated registration and filtering, structured evaluation tracks, and communication flows that handled bulk operations flawlessly. This allowed me to run job fairs and internship programs that matched more than twenty-one thousand candidates with over eight hundred companies. It reduced hiring chaos for startups and gave students a clear, structured path into meaningful opportunities.

I'm a Senior SAP Technology Specialist  over 10 years of experience specializing in ABAP, RAP, and UI5 (JavaScript). My journey began with traditional ABAP development, evolving through modern SAP technologies like Restful Application Programming (RAP), and most recently exploring SAP AI Core integration.

Over the past decade, I've worked on production SAP systems for clients like tesa SE, Mercedes-Benz, and Toyota, handling complex integrations including IDoc processing, warehouse management, and enterprise automation. I've architected solutions spanning material master extensions, production order enhancements, custom output management, and sophisticated billing systems.

My recent focus has been on intelligent automation - I'm building an n8n-based IDoc error monitoring system that connects SAP via OData and sends intelligent notifications to Microsoft Teams. column48:
Complex Enterprise Integrations:
Built dozens of SAP integrations including IDoc extensions for material master data, warehouse management enhancements, production order processing, and external system connections via REST/OData APIs for clients across automotive, chemical, and pharmaceutical industries.

Custom Output Management Systems:
Developed sophisticated Adobe Forms solutions with dynamic data binding, multi-language support, and complex business logic for printing production orders, invoices, and compliance documents.
~Project: Real-Time Production Order Integration with External Printing Services (2023-2024)
Client: Major European automotive parts manufacturer

~The Problem:
The client needed to integrate SAP with an external printing service provider to print production order documents on-demand across 12 manufacturing sites in 6 countries. The challenge was multi-layered:

~Business Context: Production orders needed to be printed within 2 minutes of creation, with documents available at the correct production line. Any delay meant workers waiting, lines stopping, and production targets missed. Cost: €5,000-10,000 per hour of downtime.

~ Technical Complexity:
- 50,000+ production orders daily across all sites
- Dynamic document types (production orders, component lists, quality specs, safety instructions)
- Multi-language requirements (German, English, Polish, Czech, Hungarian, French)
- Real-time data from multiple SAP tables needing transformation
- Legacy ABAP code that hadn't been touched in 8 years
- No standard SAP solution available

~Technical Hurdles:

1. Performance Bottleneck: Initial implementation caused system slowdowns during peak production hours. SAP could generate PDFs but the process blocked critical transactions.
   - Solution: Designed an asynchronous processing architecture using background jobs with priority queuing, separating PDF generation from order creation workflow.

2. Data Consistency: Production orders reference materials, BOMs, routings, and work centers - all of which could be updated mid-process, causing document-data mismatches.
   - Solution: Implemented a snapshot mechanism capturing all related data at order creation time, stored in custom database tables with referential integrity checks.

3. API Integration Complexity: External printing service used REST APIs that weren't natively supported by the old SAP system (ECC 6.0, not S/4HANA).
   - Solution: Built a custom HTTP client using cl_http_client with OAuth 2.0 authentication, retry logic, and comprehensive error handling. Included circuit breaker pattern to prevent cascading failures.

4. **Dynamic Form Generation:** Each production order type required different document layouts with varying data sections.
   - *Solution:* Developed a framework using Adobe Forms with dynamic form selection based on order type, material characteristics, and plant configuration. Created reusable components to avoid duplicate maintenance.

**Non-Technical Hurdles:**

1. **Stakeholder Alignment:** Production managers, IT, quality teams, and external vendor all had different priorities and timelines.
   - Solution: Ran weekly sync meetings with demo sessions. Built a monitoring dashboard showing real-time processing metrics that everyone could understand.

2. Zero-Downtime Deployment: Couldn't stop production to deploy the solution.
   - Solution: Implemented feature toggles allowing parallel operation of old and new systems. Gradually migrated plants one at a time with immediate rollback capability.

3. Documentation & Knowledge Transfer:  Client's internal team needed to maintain the solution after go-live.
   - Solution: Created comprehensive technical documentation, recorded troubleshooting videos, and conducted 3-day hands-on training sessions with real scenarios.

~ How I Succeeded:

1. Iterative Approach: Started with a single plant pilot, gathered feedback, refined, then scaled. This caught issues early when they were cheap to fix.

2. Proactive Monitoring: Built extensive logging and alerting into the solution. Created a real-time dashboard showing success rates, processing times, and error patterns - making issues visible before users reported them.

3. Performance Testing: Simulated peak load scenarios (8,000 orders/hour) in test environment to identify bottlenecks before production deployment.

4. Collaboration: Worked closely with the external printing vendor's development team, identifying API limitations early and collaborating on optimizations.

~ Results:
- Successfully processing 50,000+ documents daily across 12 sites
- Average processing time: 47 seconds from order creation to print queue
- 99.7% success rate (down from 30% initial failure rate in pilot)
- Zero production stoppages due to printing delays since go-live
- Client estimated savings: €2.3M annually from reduced downtime and eliminated manual workarounds
- Solution now being rolled out to 8 additional plants in 2025

~ Key Learnings:
This project taught me that the hardest problems in enterprise software aren't purely technical - they're about understanding business impact, managing complexity across systems and stakeholders, and designing for reliability under pressure. It's also why I'm excited about GenAI: similar problems around intelligent error handling, document generation, and system integration could be solved more elegantly with AI-powered solutions that learn and adapt rather than requiring explicit programming for every scenario.

column48:
One of the most challenging and impressive projects I worked on involved fixing a critical issue in Fynd’s BNPL product, where Safari and iOS browsers were blocking our authentication flow. Our entire repayment and onboarding experience relied on cross-origin cookies inside iframes, but Safari’s privacy restrictions were silently rejecting those cookies. This caused the OTP step to fail and users couldn’t proceed, directly impacting conversion and merchant trust.

I broke the problem down by isolating where Safari’s restrictions were interfering-eventually tracing it to cookie rejection inside the iframe. I reviewed Apple’s privacy guidelines and ran controlled tests with alternate approaches.

To make the flow reliable, I redesigned the mechanism so that the authentication state was passed through URL-safe tokens instead of relying on cross-origin cookies. I implemented secure, temporary, single-use tokens that the iframe could receive and exchange with the backend to complete the session setup.

This avoided the cookie restrictions entirely and restored the full repayment flow without compromising security. Along the way, I added fallback guards, error handling screens, and consistent UI behavior across iOS devices.

column48:
One of the most challenging yet rewarding projects I’ve worked on was my graduation project, where I created a short animated film inspired by the Silent Era, accompanied by a detailed research paper. I had a vision to creatively bring together three legendary actors of that time, showcasing their iconic styles of comedy and connecting their performances into a single seamless narrative.
The challenge was that I had no prior experience in animation, yet I wanted to attempt something ambitious. Crafting the story itself took a lot of iteration and clarity. Once the storyline was finalized, the execution became the most demanding part. I used paper cell animation, which required manually cutting each character and scene, building rigs, and moving elements frame by frame. The process was extremely time-consuming and technically exhausting, and there were moments where I doubted whether I’d be able to complete it.
Beyond the technical hurdles, there were non-technical challenges like maintaining motivation, staying consistent through weeks of repetitive work, and managing time while continuously learning new skills.
One of my favorite parts of the project was sound design. Even though the film was inspired by the silent era, the audio was what finally brought everything to life and made the narrative emotionally cohesive.
Ultimately, I succeeded by not giving up, learning on the go, and pushing through the parts that felt impossible. The project still has room for refinement, but the effort and passion I put into it were truly visible,  the power of learning by doing.

I started building early. In high school at Singapore International School (2018-2020), I co-founded Academic Reserve - a platform that helped 10,000+ IB and ISC students access curated study resources, past papers, and mentorship from top-performing alumni. We connected students with seniors who'd cracked perfect IB scores and gotten into Ivy League and top UK universities. That was my first real taste of building something that scaled.
I went to O.P. Jindal Global University for a BBA LLB (2020-2025). But I realized early that I wasn't going to practice law. While my classmates were prepping for litigation, I was running a marketing agency.
In my second year of college, I founded Zapp Marketing - a full-stack boutique agency serving D2C and SME brands. I handled everything: client acquisition, account management, and media buying strategies across Meta and Google. Built and led a team of 10-15 designers, marketers, and account managers. Implemented SOPs for execution, analytics, and crisis handling. All while attending classes and completing legal internships at Trilegal, Bombay High Court, and HN Legal.
During this same period, I completed an 11-month Advanced Certificate in Digital Marketing & Communication from MICA (via UpGrad) - so I was simultaneously studying law, running an agency, and doing a professional certification. Looking back, I don't know how I managed it, but I did.
After shutting down the agency, I joined Emami as a remote Marketing Strategist & Consultant for a year (2024-2025). Worked with their internal brand teams on digital campaigns for personal care products, introduced data-driven audience segmentation, and conducted competitive intelligence to inform strategy.
From Emami, I moved to Traya Health as a Brand Marketing Strategist, where I designed and executed a ₹25L influencer-led campaign targeting regional audiences, developed high-converting ad creatives for Meta, and led cross-functional collaboration across performance marketing, influencer relations, and content production.
While working at Traya, I started building on the side - experimenting with AI agents for enterprise use cases. That side project turned into Agent Space, an AI startup focused on document processing automation for supply chain and customs operations. We've already onboarded our first enterprise client and are processing real documents in production - extracting 95+ fields from complex customs documents with high accuracy.
I recently quit Traya to pursue Agent Space full-time. I'm a non-technical founder who's learning to build while running the company - hired a tech lead, assembled a hybrid team, and am now deep in product development. That's exactly why I'm here at 100X.
column20:
I joined 100X because I have a very specific problem: I'm a non-technical founder running an AI company, and that gap is costing me.
Right now, when my developers propose an architecture or a tech stack, I can't evaluate whether it's the right choice or overkill. When we're scoping a new product for a client, I can't accurately estimate what it should cost to build - which means I either underprice and bleed money, or overprice and lose deals. When something breaks or takes longer than expected, I can't tell if it's a genuine technical challenge or poor execution. I'm essentially flying blind in an industry I have zero background in.
What I want from this cohort:
1. Understand AI architectures and systems deeply enough to lead my team. I don't need to write production code myself - I have developers for that. But I need to understand how RAG systems work, how agents are orchestrated, what LangChain and LangGraph actually do under the hood, how vector databases fit in, what makes one architecture scalable and another a mess. Without this, I can't make informed decisions or push back when something doesn't make sense.
2. Learn to estimate product costs accurately. If a client asks me to build an AI agent for invoice processing, I need to know: what infrastructure does this require? What are the API costs? How many developer hours is this realistically? What's a fair margin? Right now I'm guessing. I want to build a mental model for pricing AI products that's grounded in actual technical understanding.
3. Build the fundamentals of how this industry works. I come from marketing and law. I understand brand strategy, client management, contracts, and growth. But I don't understand how the IT/AI industry operates - how teams are structured, how products are scoped and delivered, what "good" looks like technically. I need this foundational knowledge to run my company effectively.
4. Become capable of prototyping independently. Not to replace my team, but to move faster. If I have an idea for a new feature or product, I want to be able to build a rough working version myself before involving developers. This speeds up iteration and helps me communicate requirements better.
5. Network with builders, potentially find a technical co-founder. I'm surrounded by marketers and business people. I need to be around engineers and technical founders who are actually shipping AI products. Whether that turns into a co-founder relationship, advisor connections, or just a peer group I can learn from - that's valuable.
That's why I chose the entrepreneurship track. I'm not here to become a developer. I'm here to become a founder who can lead an AI company without being dependent on others to make every technical decision for me.
