The problem: Enterprise clients in supply chain and customs deal with massive document volumes - some processing 300-400 page documents, with 5,000+ documents annually. Each document contains critical data that needs to be extracted accurately, validated, and pushed into their systems. Manual processing is slow, expensive, and riddled with human error. Existing OCR solutions fail because these documents have inconsistent formats, mixed languages, and context-dependent fields.
Technical hurdles: I had zero coding background. Had to learn how AI systems work from scratch - understanding LLMs, prompt engineering, RAG architectures, API integrations - while simultaneously building the product. Went through multiple failed approaches before landing on a context-aware extraction system. Dealt with issues like Google API quota limits, authentication migrations (service account to OAuth), and building reliable export pipelines.
Non-technical hurdles: Hiring as a non-technical founder is brutal. Evaluated 15+ candidates for Tech Lead, learned to identify red flags (misaligned expectations, poor communication, inflated credentials). Had to let go of underperformers and restructure the team multiple times. Negotiated with enterprise clients while still figuring out the product. Managed runway while balancing speed vs. quality.
Where I am now: We've onboarded a pilot client and are in active development. Still early - the system is being built and refined. But the product has already been validated by 5-10 enterprise clients who've expressed strong interest in this solution. I have multiple product ideas in the pipeline for adjacent use cases. We're progressing, learning, and growing.

column42: Context capture, retention, reasoning and retrival ( across horizontal use cases )
column43:
While thinking through use cases, especially the direct consumer ones, I like to look at AI just as a human companion accompanying us in every task, with a similar ability to understand and synthesise context ( I mean, may not be yet, but further in the future ), but with an extraordinary ability of retention and retrieval.

We as humans are constrained in many different ways; one of the most critical ones is memory.  Humans are bound to forget things, and I don’t think that’s a bad thing either, because that’s what makes us human. Forgetting is what forces us to prioritise. This ability is what makes humans and AI the perfect team.

To unlock the kind of shared value between Humans and AI, present systems should be reimagined from the foundations. So the basic foundational shift has to be on the context capturing front, so that it can be retained, reasoned and retrieved accordingly. And yes, we could see this happening to some extent, thanks to multimodal LLMs, but still, there's a long way to go.

What I mean by this is, 

From the Systems POV,
There's still a lot of friction in the representation of context to the systems. No matter how advanced and intelligent the systems get, if the given input isn't presented well, that's of no use. So the entire interface has to be reimagined; if not, then the entire context capturing mechanisms.

From Human POV,
More importantly, the whole mechanism should align well and should be adjacent to the workflows humans operate in. If that's very distinct and isn't natural to humans, then there's a risk of losing the depth of the context. As sometimes details get lost in translation, I mean sometimes they lose the context itself as representation feels like a whole other task, a few get overwhelmed because of the adoption curve and completely neglect it, etc...
column45:

Although I am just beginning to approach problems from this perspective, and nowhere near having the expertise to do it, I hope that one day every citizen of India, especially those in Bharat, can benefit from AI to improve their lives. I aspire to contribute, even in a small way, to making this vision a reality.
column46:
Here's the idea I'm trying to build. It's still raw, and there's still a lot to think about.

Here's the question from where it started.

Why do you think QR codes are that complex? Aren't they because the information that has to be stored needs to be unique, or might be important, or because of some privacy concerns? And now thinking of it the other way, if we want some system similar to the QR, but a lot more simple, to use for just normal daily things, will it be possible? Because I think now, because of AI, we now have the context in between. So the scanner is just not a scanner, but it could also really know what might be the situation or the occurrence that's about to happen.

And here's the use case.

So, think about how we document things today — we mostly rely on digital devices like iPads or smartphones, where we type, record audio, or capture images. But if you look at the larger population, like students in India, most of them don’t have access to these devices. So, what if we could come up with an alternative that works on something as simple as paper?

Imagine using a regular piece of paper, just like we’ve been using for centuries, but now, with the help of AI, we could make it interactive. Let’s say you draw a simple symbol, like a circle or a cross. That symbol could represent a specific action, like recording a voice note. Later on, when you come back to that symbol, you could scan it with your phone, and it would pull up whatever you recorded. So, the circle could hold a voice note, an image, or whatever you decide.

The complexity comes in figuring out how the AI can recognise and understand these simple symbols, then link them to the correct actions. Plus, it has to be super accurate and quick, because if there’s any confusion about what symbol means what, the whole system could fall apart. But if we get it right, it could be a game-changer for people who don’t have access to fancy devices, making documentation much more affordable and accessible.

column48: There is a service that I designed and led implementation of at Oracle called Appmanager (SaaS Application Manager). I'll talk about it at a very high level. There were a lot of common things that every SaaS service at Oracle had to perform at a Control plane level, few integrations and orchestrations. We designed a system that does a lot of heavy lifting for these services so that things are standardised and not duplicated. Again the system needed to be highly configurable because there are differences from service to service within the commonalities. We had around 80 services onboarded onto our platform. The challenging part was to have the right balance between standardisation and customisation, supporting generic features that works for all as well as allow exceptions as in deviations from the standard for a few priority services with specific requirements. Over time, our service and onboarding to our service became complex due to the increasing set of configurations that were being added. We solved the problem by two means. We added an automation layer on top of the onboarding layer so that the onboarding process and configurations become much easier for our customers, i.e the SaaS service teams. The second one was to take the tough call and say 'No' for a few requests that would make the system unnecessarily complex. Clear guidelines on what would be supported and what wouldn't be supported and reasons behind the same were provided.

Non-technical challenges:

- Ensuring consistent tone and storytelling style

I succeeded by breaking the workflow into modular AI agents and mapping out a structured pipeline with automated triggers and checkpoints. This dramatically reduces production time while improving accuracy and consistency.

column48:
The most challenging and impressive project I’ve worked on was leading the social and content transformation during the rebrand of Amazon miniTV into Amazon MX Player**. The problem wasn’t just a name change — it was rebuilding the platform’s entire cultural positioning, audience funnel and creator ecosystem while keeping multiple flagship shows live and maintaining growth.

The first challenge was clarity. We had to merge two fundamentally different brand DNAs, reset audience expectations and communicate the shift without losing the massive organic momentum miniTV had built. On the technical side, we had to transition all social assets, metadata, content pipelines and creator workflows in real time while ensuring zero disruption to performance across Instagram, YouTube, Snapchat and WhatsApp. Non-technical hurdles were even bigger: aligning internal stakeholders, managing creator expectations, retraining agencies on the new tone and visual language, and calming audience confusion during the identity switch.

I approached the problem with a zero-to-one mindset: I built the new vertical-first content strategy, reset weekly operating cadences, onboarded the right agencies, and mobilized creators to drive cultural validation for the new brand. I designed new IP rollouts, refreshed templates, restructured deliverables, and set performance guardrails to keep engagement high during the transition. We leveraged data aggressively — testing formats, hooks, thumbnails and release timings to stabilize retention and rebuild affinity quickly.

We ultimately succeeded because we treated the rebrand as a launch, not an announcement. The shift landed smoothly, audience sentiment stayed positive, and flagship titles like Hip Hop India S2, Rise & Fall, Playground and Jamnapaar continued to scale without losing their momentum. The ecosystem became stronger, more creator-aligned and more culturally relevant than before.

This project forced me to combine strategic clarity, technical coordination, creator management and high-velocity execution — and it’s still the most complex and rewarding challenge I’ve led.
